front: Figure: the second President of Indonesia, ruling from 1967 to 1998 after succeeding Sukarno
Figure: a longtime military dictator of Indonesia who ruled for 32 years
Event: a failed 1965 coup in which six army generals were killed and thrown down a well
Figure: blamed the 30 September Movement coup on the Communist Party (PKI) and, backed by the CIA, used it to seize power
Figure: used the coup's fallout to replace Sukarno's "Guided Democracy" with his own "New Order" government
Event: a 1966 decree that granted Suharto the power to do whatever he deemed "necessary," used to suppress the Communist Party (PKI)
Figure: led a year-long anti-communist purge that killed an estimated one to one-and-a-half million suspected communists
Figure: his official narrative of the 1965 coup was challenged by historians Ruth McVey and Benedict Anderson's "Cornell Paper"
Figure: his rise to power is the subject of John Roosa's book Pretext for Mass Murder
Figure: formally took power as president six years after the Supersemar, when his "Functional Group" took full control
Figure: brutally suppressed an independence movement in Aceh
Institution: this special forces unit was employed by Suharto in his rise to power and later ended the hijacking of Garuda Flight 206 by the group Komando Jihad
Institution: Suharto's government, whose name was contrasted with Sukarno's "Old" version
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Institution: the "dual function" (dwifungsi) policy gave the military (ABRI) a built-in role in both security and civilian government, including reserved parliamentary seats
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Figure: introduced "dwifungsi" to reject General Abdul Haris Nasution's proposed "middle way" for the military's role
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Institution: founded by Suharto's subordinate Ali Murtopo, and adopted the Pancasila philosophy originally associated with the Communist Party (PKI)
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Figure: headed the Golkar Party before formally taking power as president
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Figure: pursued "indigenization" of the state philosophy Pancasila to eliminate Western influence
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Event: a 1980 petition protesting Suharto's declaration of Pancasila as the nation's "single principle"
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Institution: this security force, commanded under Suharto, arrested the founder of the human rights group Tapol
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Event: Suharto's Aspri group of personal advisors was disbanded after this riot, triggered by a state visit from Japanese Prime Minister Kakuei Tanaka
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Figure: the Malari Incident led him to limit foreign investment and promote local ownership of firms
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Event: a plebiscite Suharto's government held to circumvent the New York Agreement and annex West Papua in the east
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Figure: promoted "pribumi" (indigenous) interests, similar to Malaysia's bumiputera policy, while targeting the ethnic Chinese minority
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Event: Suharto's regime sent red-shirted attackers to storm the headquarters of the opposition party in this 1996 incident
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Figure: his legitimacy was challenged by the PDI, led by Megawati Sukarnoputri, the daughter of his predecessor Sukarno
work: The New Order: Ideology and Repression

front: Figure: liberalized the economy with the guidance of American-educated technocrats nicknamed the "Berkeley Mafia"
Figure: undertook investment programs under five-year development plans called REPELITA
Figure: a group of his economic advisors were hired after presentations at the SESKOAD army college
Figure: opponents coined this acronym meaning "corruption, collusion, and nepotism" to describe his economy
Figure: kickbacks from awarding state monopolies were disguised as donations to yayasans that ostensibly built rural schools and hospitals
Figure: his crony Bob Hasan, the "plywood king," led negotiations with the fraudulent Canadian gold mining firm Bre-X
Figure: exposure of a purchase of dilapidated East German warships led his regime to ban magazines like Editor and Tempo
Institution: this conglomerate, Suharto's regime's "business pillar," was targeted when rioters burned ethnic Chinese businesses during his fall
Figure: invaded East Timor in 1975, beginning a bloody occupation that lasted until 1999
work: The Indonesian Occupation of East Timor

front: Event: FRETILIN and its military wing FALINTIL resisted this occupation, which had U.S. support from Henry Kissinger
work: The Indonesian Occupation of East Timor

front: Event: a minor incursion preceding the full invasion, code-named Operation Seroja, killed a group of Australian journalists known by this name
work: The Indonesian Occupation of East Timor

front: Event: Indonesian soldiers killed around 250 pro-independence protesters at this 1991 massacre in a Dili cemetery, filmed by journalists including Max Stahl
back: Santa Cruz massacre (Indonesian occupation of East Timor)
work: The Indonesian Occupation of East Timor

front: Event: Carlos Filipe Ximenes Belo and Jose Ramos-Horta won the 1996 Nobel Peace Prize for trying to resolve this conflict
work: The Indonesian Occupation of East Timor

front: Work: Michele Turner collected oral testimonies from victims of the Indonesian occupation of East Timor in this book
work: The Indonesian Occupation of East Timor

front: Event: the occupied territory finally achieved independence in 2002
work: The Indonesian Occupation of East Timor

front: Event: this crisis, which began when Thailand's baht collapsed, sent Indonesia's rupiah plummeting and helped trigger Suharto's downfall
Figure: was forced to resign after a deadly riot at Trisakti University
Figure: resigned in 1998, ending 32 years in power, and was succeeded by this man, who launched the "Reformasi" reform period
